INFS 3822

Week 3 Seminar

Structured Data Analysis using AI
Dr. Jason Xianghua Wu




         School of Information Systems & Technology Management
         UNSW Business School
 Week 3 Seminar Recap

                   Structured data in the business context


               Machine Learning for Structured Data Analysis


                   Pre-processing Structured Data for ML


                 Deep Learning for Structured Data Analysis


           AI-Driven Structured Data Analysis to solve Business Problems

                     Finance            Credit, Lending, and Mortgage Sector
 Structured data in the business context




                                          3
 Structured data
• Structured data refers to data that is organized in a specific
  format, such as a table or spreadsheet
              Structured data                Unstructured data



                                                        Text, Photos
                                                          & Videos


     Emojis       Location      Sensor
      Likes         data         Data
     Shares        (GPS)

                                                                       4
 Structured data collection and analysis
• Easier to analyse and extract insights from structured data
  compared to unstructured data (e.g. text, images, audio).
• Data warehousing and ETL (extract, transform, load) processes
  are often used to collect, store, and prepare structured data for
  analysis.




                                                  ML

                        Extract
                       Transform
                         Load
                                                                      5
 Examples of structured data in a business context

• Customer data, including purchase history, demographics (age,
  gender, income, education level, and location), and segmentation
  information.
• Sales data, including revenue, units sold, discounts, and return
  information.
• Inventory data, including stock levels, and supplier information.
• Financial data, including income statements, balance sheets, and
  cash flow statements.
• Marketing data, including website analytics, social media metrics,
  and email campaign performance.
                                                                   6
 Examples of structured data in a business context

• Human resources data, including employee information, payroll
  data, and performance metrics.
• Supply chain data, including logistics information, shipping data,
  and production data.
• Operational data, including machine data, sensor data, and
  performance metrics from production lines.
• Logistics data, tracking information of orders and deliveries.




                                                                       7
 Machine Learning
for Structured Data Analysis




                               Source: https://xkcd.com/1838/
                                                                8
 AI-driven Business Analytics using structured data

• Machine learning algorithms can be applied to structured data to
  make predictions and identify patterns.
• By utilizing structured data, businesses can make data-driven
  decisions and improve their operations and performance.


              Regression            Classification                 Ranking




                       Clustering              Anomaly Detection



                                                                             9
 Linear Regression
• Objective: Find the line of best fit through a set of data points in
  order to predict a numeric value
• Advantages: Simplicity and ease of interpretation
• Disadvantages: Assumption of linearity and a lack of ability to
  model more complex relationships in the data
• Business applications
 • Predict sales based on advertising spend
 • Predict the relationship between product prices and demand.



                                                                         11
 Example




          12
 Logistic Regression
• Objective: Used for classification problems by modelling the probability of a
  binary outcome given a set of input features
• Advantages: Simplicity, ease of interpretation, and the ability to handle non-
  linearly separable data through the use of regularization
• Disadvantages: Assumption of independence among features and its limited
  ability to model complex relationships in the data
• Business applications
 • Customer churn prediction
 • Determining the likelihood of a customer making a purchase based on their
   browsing behavior
 • Predict the probability of a customer responding to a marketing campaign.
                                                                                   13
 Logistic Function




                    14
 Decision Trees
• Objective: Create a model that can predict the value of a target variable by
  learning simple decision rules inferred from the data features
• Advantages: Interpretability, ability to handle both continuous and categorical
  data, and the ability to handle multi-output problems
• Disadvantages: Tendency to overfit and their instability, meaning that small
  changes in the data can result in a very different decision tree
• Business applications
 • Customer segmentation
 • Determining which product to recommend to a customer based on their browsing
   history
 • Identifying which customers are most likely to default on a loan
                                                                                  15
 Random Forest
• Objective: Create an ensemble of decision trees to improve the predictive
  performance of individual decision trees by decreasing overfitting and
  increasing generalisation
• Advantages: Ability to handle large amounts of data and a wide range of
  features, and ability to handle both continuous and categorical variables.
• Disadvantages: Complexity and interpretability. Require more computational
  resources to train and evaluate
• Business applications
 • Fraud detection
 • Predict which customers are most likely to respond to a marketing campaign
 • Identify which products are most likely to sell well based on historical sales data
                                                                                         16
 Pre-processing Structured Data for ML
Transform the data to become suitable for ML models




                                                      18
 Pre-processing of Structured Data for ML

• Data Cleaning
 •   Handling Missing Values
 •   Removing Outliers
• Data Transformation
 •   Normalization/Min-Max Scaling
 •   Standardization/Z-score normalization
 •   Log Transformation




                                             19
 Pre-processing of Structured Data for ML

• Feature Encoding
 •     One-hot encoding
       Converting each categorical value into a new categorical column and assigns a 1 or 0
       (True/False) value to the column.

     Item             Colour             Item        Colour_Red    Colour_Blue   Colour_Green
     Item 1            Red               Item 1          1             0              0
     Item 2            Blue              Item 2          0             1              0
     Item 3            Green             Item 3          0             0              1
     Item 4            Red               Item 4          1             0              0



                                         Item          Colour
                                         Item 1          1
                                                                  Red colour should not be
                                         Item 2          2
                                                                  considered “inferior” to
                                         Item 3          3        Green colour
                                         Item 4          1
                                                                                                20
 Pre-processing of Structured Data for ML

• Feature Encoding
 •   Label Encoding
     Converting each categorical value in a column to a number. Useful for encoding
     categorical data with ordinal relationships.


              Participant       Height            Participant     Height
              Participant 1      Tall             Participant 1     3
              Participant 2     Short             Participant 2     1
              Participant 3    Medium             Participant 3     2
              Participant 4      Tall             Participant 4     3




                                                                                      21
 Pre-processing of Structured Data for ML

• Feature Engineering
 •   Feature Creation
     Creation of new input variables based on existing data.


       Customer       Purchase Date         Customer     Weekend Purchase   Days since last purchase
       Customer 1      2024-01-01           Customer 1          0                     43
       Customer 2      2024-01-13           Customer 2          1                     35
       Customer 3      2024-02-02           Customer 3          0                     130
       Customer 4      2024-02-18           Customer 4          1                     55




                                                                                                       22
 Pre-processing of Structured Data for ML

• Feature Engineering
 •   Feature Selection
     Selecting the most important features to train the model on.
     Techniques include:
         •   Filter Methods: selection based on statistical measures (e.g. correlation coefficients, Chi-
             square test, mutual information, Fisher’s score)
         •   Wrapper Methods: selection of a set of features as a search problem, where different
             combinations are prepared, evaluated, and compared with each other. (e.g. forward
             selection, backward elimination, and recursive feature elimination)
         •   Embedded Methods: selection as part of the model training. Typically built into the ML
             algorithms such as decision trees or ensemble methods like Random Forests and Gradient
             Boosting Machines (GBM)


                                                                                                       23
 Pre-processing of Structured Data for ML

• Feature Engineering
 •   Dimensionality Reduction
     Reduce the number of features in data to simplify models and reduce computation time
        •        Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
        •        Autoencoders




            V1       V2    V3    V4     V5    V6      V7   V8   V9   V10   V11   V12




                                        D1    D2      D3   D4

Exercise   Structured data classification using logistic regression

  • Objective: Train a model in Python or R to classify structured data into a
    Boolean outcome
  • Data: any CSV file that has one field/variable as a Boolean outcome variable
    to predict.
    • We will use a synthetic dataset that has 24 columns (variables), one of which is a Boolean
      to be used as the outcome variable (to predict).
  • Model type: Logistic Regression
  • Environment:

                                                                             or



                                                                                               27
 Hands-On
Exercise   Introduction to Python / R

  • What is Python/R
  • Why Python/R for ML: capabilities
  • How to use it? Live demo of Google Colab




                                               28
 Hands-On
Exercise   Introduction to Python / R
  Python and R are both popular among data scientists for a number of reasons:
  •   Versatility: Both Python and R are versatile programming languages that can be used for a
      wide variety of tasks, including data cleaning, exploration, visualization, and modeling.
  •   Large and active communities: Both languages have large and active communities of
      users and developers, which means that there are many resources and tools available for
      learning and working with the languages.
  •   Rich ecosystem of libraries and frameworks: Both languages have a rich ecosystem of
      libraries and frameworks for data science and machine learning, including NumPy and
      Pandas for data manipulation, Matplotlib and ggplot for data visualization, scikit-learn and
      TensorFlow for machine learning, and many more.
  •   Good for prototyping: Both languages are easy to learn and use, and have an intuitive
      and interactive development environment, which makes them well-suited for prototyping
      and exploring data quickly.


                                                                                                 29
 Hands-On
Exercise   Introduction to Python / R
  Python and R are both popular among data scientists for a number of reasons:
  •   Power and performance: Python is a general-purpose language, it's easy to use and
      versatile, and it offers high-performance libraries such as NumPy and Pandas to process
      large amount of data. R is developed mainly for statistical analysis with a powerful data
      visualization capabilities, that's why it's commonly used among statisticians and data
      analysts.
  •   Industry and academic: Both languages are widely used both in academia and industry,
      Python is well-known as a versatile and powerful general-purpose programming language,
      which makes it also a choice for software development, web development and more. R is
      more specialized in statistical analysis and data visualization, which makes it a must have
      for data scientists and statisticians.
  •   Popularity: Python and R are both very popular in data science and machine learning,
      Python has been growing faster in popularity in recent years, but both are well-established
      languages with many resources and tools available.


                                                                                                  30
 Hands-On
Exercise   How to use Python / R in Google Colab?
  • Google Colab, a product from Google Research.
  • Colab allows you to write and execute R or Python code through the
    browser, and is especially well suited to machine learning, data analysis and
    education.
  • Requires no setup to use, while providing access free of charge to computing
    resources including GPUs.
  • To use Google Colab, you need to be signed-in to a Google account (you
    can create one for free)
  • Visit https://colab.research.google.com/

                                                                                               33
 Hands-On
Exercise   How to use Python / R in Google Colab?
  • In Google Colab, you can create Python or R notebooks to run analytics.
  • Notebooks can be created from scratch, or uploaded as .ipynb files.
  • Two notebook files are available on Moodle:
    • Week3_Hands_On_Exercise_Python.ipynb
    • Week3_Hands_On_Exercise_R.ipynb
  • They contain Python and R code respectively for this Hands-On exercise.




                                                                              34
 Hands-On
Exercise   Structured data classification using logistic regression




                                                                  35
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   import pandas as pd                                         # Load the data
   from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split        data <- read.csv("data_exercise1.csv")
   from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
   from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score                  # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)
                                                               X <- data[,-1]
   # Load the data                                             y <- data[,ncol(data)]
   data = pd.read_csv(”data_exercise1.csv")
                                                               # Scale the input data
   # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)            X <- scale(X)
   X = data.drop("outcome", axis=1)
   y = data["outcome"]                                         # Split the data into training and testing sets
                                                               set.seed(123) # For reproducibility
   # Preprocess the data (scaling)                             n <- nrow(X)
   scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X)              splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace
   X = scaler.transform(X)                                     = FALSE)
                                                               X_train <- X[splitIndex,]
   # Split the data into training and testing sets             y_train <- y[splitIndex]
   X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y,   X_test <- X[-splitIndex,]
   test_size=0.2)                                              y_test <- y[-splitIndex]




                                                                                                                          36
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   import pandas as pd                                      # Load the data
   from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split     data <- read.csv("data.csv")
   from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
   from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score               # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)
                                                            X <- data[,-1]
   # Load the data                                          y <- data[,ncol(data)]
   data = pd.read_csv("data.csv")
                                                            # Scale the input data
   # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)         X <- scale(X)
   X = data.drop("outcome", axis=1)
   y = data["outcome"]                                      # Split the data into training and testing sets
                                                            set.seed(123) # For reproducibility
   # Preprocess the data (scaling)                          n <- nrow(X)
   scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X)           splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace
   X = scaler.transform(X)                                  = FALSE)
   First, we need to load the necessary libraries. This includes:
                                                            X_train <- X[splitIndex,]
   # Split the data into training and testing sets          y_train <- y[splitIndex]
   Python:
   X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y,X_test <- X[-splitIndex,]
   • The pandas library, which is used for data manipulation and analysis.
   test_size=0.2)                                           y_test <- y[-splitIndex]

   • The train_test_split function to split the data into training and testing sets.
   • The LogisticRegression class, which is a linear model for binary classification
   • The accuracy_score function which is used to measure the performance of a classifier model.
   R: For logistic regression in R, we do not need any libraries other than those loaded by default.
                                                                                                                       37
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   import pandas as pd                                         # Load the data
   from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split        data <- read.csv("data.csv")
   from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
   from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score                  # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)
                                                               X <- data[,-1]
   # Load the data                                             y <- data[,ncol(data)]
   data = pd.read_csv("data.csv")
                                                               # Scale the input data
   # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)            X <- scale(X)
   X = data.drop("outcome", axis=1)
   y = data["outcome"]                                         # Split the data into training and testing sets
                                                               set.seed(123) # For reproducibility
   # Preprocess the data (scaling)                             n <- nrow(X)
   scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X)              splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace
   X = scaler.transform(X)                                     = FALSE)
                                                               X_train <- X[splitIndex,]
   # Split the data into training and testing sets             y_train <- y[splitIndex]
   X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y,   X_test <- X[-splitIndex,]
   test_size=0.2)                                              y_test <- y[-splitIndex]
   Next, we load the dataset from a CSV file
   Python: data = pd.read_csv("data.csv") reads the data from 'data.csv' file and loads into data variable using
   the read_csv function from the pandas library (aliased as pd).
   R: data <- read.csv("data.csv") reads the data from 'data.csv' file and loads into data variable using the
   read.csv function.
                                                                                                                          38
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   import pandas as pd                                         # Load the data
   from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split        data <- read.csv("data.csv")
   from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
   from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score                  # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)
                                                               X <- data[,-1]
   # Load the data                                             y <- data[,ncol(data)]
   data = pd.read_csv("data.csv")
                                                               # Scale the input data
   # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)            X <- scale(X)
   X = data.drop(”Class", axis=1)
   y = data["Class"]                                           # Split the data into training and testing sets
                                                               set.seed(123) # For reproducibility
   # Preprocess the data (scaling)                             n <- nrow(X)
   scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X)              splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace
   X = scaler.transform(X)                                     = FALSE)
                                                               X_train <- X[splitIndex,]
   # Split the data into training and testing sets             y_train <- y[splitIndex]
   X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y,   X_test <- X[-splitIndex,]
   test_size=0.2)
   Split the data into inputs and outputs. The inputs consists of all available variables (columns) in the data
                                                               y_test <- y[-splitIndex]

   except “Class”. The output is the variable “Class” itself.
   Python: data.drop(”Class", axis=1) and y = data[”Class"] takes the input and output columns from the loaded
   dataframe. Here the “Class” column is taken as output and rest are taken as input.
   R: X <- data[,-1] and y <- data[,ncol(data)] takes the input and output columns from the loaded dataframe.
   Here the last column is taken as output and rest are taken as input.                                                   39
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   import pandas as pd                                         # Load the data
   from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split        data <- read.csv("data.csv")
   from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
   from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score                  # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)
                                                               X <- data[,-1]
   # Load the data                                             y <- data[,ncol(data)]
   data = pd.read_csv("data.csv")
                                                               # Scale the input data
   # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y)            X <- scale(X)
   X = data.drop("outcome", axis=1)
   y = data["outcome"]                                         # Split the data into training and testing sets
                                                               set.seed(123) # For reproducibility
   # Preprocess the data (scaling)                             n <- nrow(X)
   scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X)              splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace
   X = scaler.transform(X)                                     = FALSE)
                                                               X_train <- X[splitIndex,]
   # Split the data into training and testing sets             y_train <- y[splitIndex]
   X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y,   X_test <- X[-splitIndex,]
   test_size=0.2)                                              y_test <- y[-splitIndex]




   Scale the input data to ensure that all the input features are on the same scale. It standardizes the data by
   subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation.
                                                                                                                          40
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   # Preprocess the data (scaling)                              # Scale the input data
   scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X)               X <- scale(X)
   X = scaler.transform(X)
                                                                # Split the data into training and testing sets
   # Split the data into training and testing sets              set.seed(123) # For reproducibility
   X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y,    n <- nrow(X)
   test_size=0.2)                                               splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace
                                                                = FALSE)
                                                                X_train <- X[splitIndex,]
                                                                y_train <- y[splitIndex]
                                                                X_test <- X[-splitIndex,]
                                                                y_test <- y[-splitIndex]


   Split the data into training and testing sets. The training set will be used to train the model, while the testing
   set will be used to test the accuracy of the model.
   Python: Split the input and output data using the train_test_split function into training and testing set with
   test_size =0.2 meaning that the testing set corresponds to 20% of the data.
   R: splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace = FALSE) allows to split the input and output data
   into training and testing set with size = 0.8 * n (the training dataset is 80% of the size of the dataset, no being
   the number of rows).
   X_train <- X[splitIndex,], y_train <- y[splitIndex], X_test <- X[-splitIndex,] and y_test <- y[-splitIndex] split the
   input and output data into training and testing set                                                                     41
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   # Train a logistic regression model on the training data   # Train a logistic regression model on the training data
   model = LogisticRegression()                               model <- glm(y_train ~., data = X_train, family =
   model.fit(X_train, y_train)                                binomial())

   # Make predictions on the testing data                     # Make predictions on the testing data
   y_pred = model.predict(X_test)                             y_pred <- predict(model, newdata = X_test, type =
                                                              "response")
   # Calculate the accuracy of the model                      y_pred <- ifelse(y_pred > 0.5, 1, 0)
   accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
   print("Accuracy:", accuracy)                               # Calculate the accuracy of the model
                                                              accuracy <- mean(y_pred == y_test)
                                                              print(paste0("Accuracy: ", accuracy))




   Create a logistic regression model and train it using the training dataset
   Python: model = LogisticRegression() creates an instance of the LogisticRegression model.
   model.fit(X_train, y_train) trains the logistic regression model using the training data.
   R: model <- glm(y_train ~., data = X_train, family = binomial()) trains the logistic regression model using the
   glm function, with the formula y_train ~., data=X_train, family = binomial(). The formula is used to specify
   the outcome column, independent variables and the type of model.                                                      42
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   # Train a logistic regression model on the training data   # Train a logistic regression model on the training data
   model = LogisticRegression()                               model <- glm(y_train ~., data = X_train, family =
   model.fit(X_train, y_train)                                binomial())

   # Make predictions on the testing data                     # Make predictions on the testing data
   y_pred = model.predict(X_test)                             y_pred <- predict(model, newdata = X_test, type =
                                                              "response")
   # Calculate the accuracy of the model                      y_pred <- ifelse(y_pred > 0.5, 1, 0)
   accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
   print("Accuracy:", accuracy)                               # Calculate the accuracy of the model
                                                              accuracy <- mean(y_pred == y_test)
                                                              print(paste0("Accuracy: ", accuracy))




   Makes predictions on the testing data.
   Python: model.predict function is used to predict the outcome of the testing data (X_test).
   R: y_pred <- predict(model, newdata = X_test, type = "response") makes predictions on the testing data
   using predict function, with newdata = X_test and type = "response" to get the probability of outcome.
   y_pred <- ifelse(y_pred > 0.5, 1, 0) threshold the probability values to get binary values, if probability is
   greater than 0.5 then it's 1 otherwise 0
                                                                                                                         43
 Hands-On
Exercise     Example of logistic regression model in Python / R

   # Train a logistic regression model on the training data   # Train a logistic regression model on the training data
   model = LogisticRegression()                               model <- glm(y_train ~., data = X_train, family =
   model.fit(X_train, y_train)                                binomial())

   # Make predictions on the testing data                     # Make predictions on the testing data
   y_pred = model.predict(X_test)                             y_pred <- predict(model, newdata = X_test, type =
                                                              "response")
   # Calculate the accuracy of the model                      y_pred <- ifelse(y_pred > 0.5, 1, 0)
   accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
   print("Accuracy:", accuracy)                               # Calculate the accuracy of the model
                                                              accuracy <- mean(y_pred == y_test)
                                                              print(paste0("Accuracy: ", accuracy))




   Calculate the accuracy of the model by comparing the predictions with the true values in y_test
   Python: accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred) calculates the accuracy of the model. print allows to
   display the calculated accuracy.
   R: accuracy <- mean(y_pred == y_test) calculates the accuracy of the model by comparing the predictions
   with the true values in y_test.
   print(paste0("Accuracy: ", accuracy)) prints the accuracy of the model.
                                                                                                                         44
 Deep Learning for
Structured Data Analysis

                           Deep Neural Networks




                                                  45
 Deep vs. Traditional Machine Learning.
• Traditional ML: Statistical analysis to predict outcomes
 • e.g. linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees, etc.
 • Requires extensive feature engineering
 • Typically handles less complex problems
 • Offers greater interpretability
 • Requires less computational power
• Deep Learning: artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns
 • Inspired by the structure and function of the brain's neural networks
 • Minimizes the need for feature engineering
 • Capable of handling more complex problems
 • ”Black box" models makes them less transparent and harder to explain
 • Requires significant computational resources
                                                                             46
 Deep Learning Models for Structured Data
• Inspired by the brain's neural networks, organised in layers of neurons




                                                     Source: http://neuralnetworksanddeeplearning.com/chap5.html




                                                                                                                   47
 Deep Learning Models for Structured Data
• Weights and biases in a neural network
 • Goal: convert an input into an output
 • Weights: numerical parameters determine how strongly each neuron affects the others.
 • Biases: additional parameter that is added to the weighted sum (similar to an intercept)




                       Source: https://towardsdatascience.com/whats-the-role-of-weights-and-bias-in-a-neural-network-4cf7e9888a0f   48
 Deep Learning Models for Structured Data
• Fully connected Neural Networks
 • Basic form of neural network
 • Each neuron in one layer is connected to all neurons in the next layer
 • Versatile and can be used for a wide range of tasks
• Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) / Long Short-Term Memory Networks (LSTMs)
 • Well-suited for time-series data
 • Capture patterns across time steps
 • Ideal for forecasting and sequence prediction tasks
• Transformers
 • Originally designed for natural language processing
 • Attention mechanisms to weigh the importance of different parts of the data
 • Highly effective for complex relationships within the data

                                                                                 49
 Deep Learning Models for Structured Data
• Transfer learning and fine-tuning
 • Leverage/Reuse a pretrained model as starting point for a model on a different task
     • Select a Pretrained Model
     • Modify the Model for your specific task (e.g. change the final layer(s) of the model to match the
       number of outputs required for the new task)
     • Freeze the Weights of earlier layers
     • Train the Model on the new data




                                                                                                           50
 Deep Learning Models for Structured Data
• Ensemble methods
 • Approach: combine multiple models to improve the overall performance, accuracy, and
   robustness of predictions
 • Principle: a group of weak models can be combined to form a strong model
 • Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) – e.g. Random Forest
    •   Combine multiple models of the same type trained on different subsets of the same training data, selected
        with replacement.
    •   The final output is obtained by averaging the predictions (for regression tasks) or by majority voting (for
        classification tasks)
 • Boosting – e.g. Gradient Boosting, XGBoost
    •   Train models sequentially where each model attempts to correct the errors made by the previous models.
    •   The final prediction is a weighted sum of all the models' predictions (model weighted are based on their
        accuracy).

 Artificial Intelligence in Finance

Investment Banking
• Goal: assists companies and governments in raising capital
• Challenge: Investment banks need to extract insights from large and complex
  datasets to make informed business decisions.
• AI can help investment bankers to:
 • Analyse market data and identify profitable investment opportunities
 • Automate repetitive tasks such as data entry and financial modelling
 • Identify patterns and trends in financial data that may indicate potential risks or
   opportunities
 • Optimize trading strategies and make more accurate predictions about market movements
 • Automate compliance and regulatory tasks, helping banks to reduce the risk of non-
   compliance
